name: win64c-guard-registration

findings.summary: Registers one cleanup/runtime callback slot when the guard byte is enabled.
evidence: Checks byte guard 0x140C3618F and falls back to 0x1407C45DC if it is zero.
Calls the indirect import at 0x140802648, decrements the qword counter at 0x140C36190, and stores the returned pointer into virtual callback array 0x140C55EA0[index].
The locale default-object publisher at 0x1407B6150 is the direct caller observed in this pass, tying this thunk to the object cleanup registration path.

findings.summary: Four tiny non-pdata thunks dispatch to imported guard/lock/runtime helpers.
notes: These entries are packed between pdata-backed functions and are not individual runtime-function starts.
The caller cluster around 0x1407B62EC..0x1407B63D0 should be treated as the next local guard-state constructor/destructor worklist.

findings.summary: Late-resolves selected kernel32 exports and stores the resolved function pointers in globals.
notes: This function is adjacent to the guard-registration thunk but has no direct caller in the scanned direct-call target set.
The call pattern is LoadLibrary/GetProcAddress-like: one import for the module name and three calls through 0x140802730 for export names.

findings.summary: Probes/acquires a process-global owner slot using the current thread identity and locked CMPXCHG.
evidence: Calls 0x1407B8E90 first and returns AL=0 immediately when that check fails.
Reads GS:[0x30], loads the qword at TEB+0x8 into RCX, and loops on CMPXCHG.LOCK [0x140C55FE0], RCX.
Returns AL=1 only when the existing owner matches the current thread/list pointer; otherwise the successful acquisition path returns AL=0.

findings.summary: Neighbor helpers are not tiny guard thunks; they fan out into allocation/string/runtime helpers and remain a follow-up worklist.
evidence: 0x1407B7A14 has one direct caller at 0x1407A8D56 and calls 0x1407B7EC0.
0x1407B7BC8 has four direct callers and 14 internal calls, including 0x1407CD068 allocator-like calls and indirect imports at 0x140802778, 0x140802638, and 0x140802758.

0x1407B7EC0 has one direct caller from 0x1407B7A14 and 15 internal calls, including repeated 0x1407D64D8, allocator-like 0x1407CD068, and indirect imports at 0x140802628, 0x140802778, and 0x140802630.

tooling_updates.summary: Added 0F B0/B1 CMPXCHG decoding with LOCK-prefix mnemonic support and memory-target annotations.
tooling_updates.client_case: 0x1407B824B now decodes as CMPXCHG.LOCK [0x140C55FE0], RCX instead of an unknown instruction.

tooling_updates.summary: Added LOCK + optional REX + two-byte RIP-relative CMPXCHG reference extraction.
tooling_updates.client_case: 0x1407B824B now contributes a rip-relative-cmpxchg-lock hit to target 0x140C55FE0.

tooling_updates.summary: Added focused PE tests for locked CMPXCHG disassembly and address-reference extraction.

validation.qword_warning_note: 0x140C55EA0 and 0x140C55FE0 are virtual-only zero-initialized data ranges, so read-qwords reports expected file-backed read warnings.

next_worklist: Map the caller cluster 0x1407B62EC, 0x1407B6330, 0x1407B636C, and 0x1407B63A8 to name the guard enter/exit/increment/decrement semantics precisely.
Resolve the imported thunk targets around 0x140802478..0x140802490 and 0x140802648 using import-table names instead of slot-only labels.
Continue expanding the larger neighbor helpers 0x1407B7BC8 and 0x1407B7EC0 only after the guard-state constructor/destructor cluster is labeled.
